rationale: The citing paper repeatedly treats Keller, Lipkus, and Rimer (2003) as a central prior finding in the affect-framing literature, especially the claim that negative affective appeals are generally more persuasive with gain framing. It then positions its own contribution as identifying boundary conditions to that result by separating guilt from shame and testing coping-based fit with gain versus loss frames. This citation is used in theory framing and contribution claims, not just as a background list mention.
relation_description: The citing paper refines Keller et al. (2003) by qualifying the broad negative-affect/gain-frame advantage with discrete-emotion boundary conditions. It argues and shows that guilt aligns with gain frames, but shame can be more effective with loss frames due to different coping orientations.
